text: No studies have properly estimated causal effects between ACWR and injury, so manipulating ACWR to change injury rates remains conjecture and overinterpretation of available data.
The ACWR fails to properly normalize the numerator by the denominator even when acute and chronic loads are uncoupled, making it an inaccurate metric.
The ACWR is not consistently or unidirectionally related to injury risk, undermining its use as a predictive tool for training load management.
The statistical properties of ratios make the ACWR an inaccurate metric that adds noise and creates statistical artifacts, complicating interpretation for practical applications.
There is no evidence supporting the use of ACWR in training-load-management systems or for training recommendations aimed at reducing injury risk.
Recommending manipulation of ACWR to alter injury likelihood assumes a causal effect that has not been established, introducing problematic conceptual and methodological issues.
